cue: How do you say someone twists everything to their own advantage?

es.text: Arrimar el ascua a su sardina
es.literal: to draw the ember to one's own sardine
es.meaning: To turn every situation to one's own advantage.
es.example: Siempre arrima el ascua a su sardina: cualquier acuerdo acaba beneficiándole.
es.exampleTranslation: He always works it round to his own benefit — every deal ends up favouring him.

it.literal: to draw the water to one's own mill
it.meaning: To turn every situation to one's own advantage.
it.example: Tira sempre l'acqua al suo mulino: ogni accordo finisce per favorirlo.
it.exampleTranslation: He always works it round to his own benefit — every deal ends up favouring him.

bridge.note: Identical grammar, different economies. Both drag a resource toward a thing that is theirs — and the resource tells you what each culture pictures as work. Spain drags EMBERS toward a sardine on the fire: one person, one supper, immediate. Italy diverts WATER toward a mill: property, machinery, a livelihood turning. Selfishness at the scale of a meal versus selfishness at the scale of an industry.

interference.es_to_it: The Italian resource is water and the destination is a mill — embers and sardines will only puzzle. And it is tirare, to pull, not arrimare.
interference.it_to_es: 'Llevar el agua a su molino' does exist in Spanish and is understood, but the vivid refrán is the sardine on the coals.

review.notes: ES side discovered in a Spanish idioms book Joe is reading, 2026-07-21 (discovery only; every gloss and example here authored fresh). IT counterpart chosen by Claude from recall — UNVERIFIED. Same skeleton, two economies — one of the cleanest bridges in the bank.
